Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex A sophisticated cyber attack targeting water and wastewater systems involves extensive reconnaissance, resource development, and multi-stage infiltration to achieve impact. Expert attackers utilize advanced techniques to maintain persistence, escalate privileges, evade defenses, access credentials, discover network configurations, move laterally, collect sensitive data, exfiltrate information, and ultimately disrupt operations.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Command & Control Server, Network Traffic, File System, Application Log, Target System Artifact, Logon Session
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: General Access Method, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Response Metadata (Mechanism), Web Shell, Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism, Hide Artifacts, Mimikatz Tool, System Network Configuration Discovery, Application Log Content (Mechanism), Module Load (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers conduct active scanning to map the network topology of the water and wastewater systems, identifying vulnerable points and potential entry vectors.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: Establishment of command and control servers across multiple jurisdictions to facilitate covert communication channels for the attack.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 439 Name: Manipulation During Distribution
stage.description: Infiltration via compromised software updates to water treatment management systems, exploiting trust in the software supply chain.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121 Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces
stage.description: Exploitation of known vulnerabilities in web interfaces of Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) systems.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 650 Name: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: Maintaining unauthorized access by installing a backdoor on compromised servers, ensuring continuous presence within the network.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Gaining elevated permissions within the system to manipulate critical processes and services.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 498 Name: Probe iOS Screenshots
stage.description: Concealing malicious activities and tools to avoid detection by security monitoring solutions.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226 Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: Stealing credentials to move laterally within the network undetected.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: Mapping out the network to identify key assets and potential pathways for lateral movement.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Using stolen credentials to move throughout the network and establish further footholds.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 277 Name: Data Interchange Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Gathering sensitive operational data related to water and wastewater systems.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 24 Name: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Transferring collected data to attacker-controlled servers securely via established command and control channels.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 276 Name: Inter-component Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: Disrupting operations to cause physical damage or service interruption to the water and wastewater systems.

Scenario.summary: The Indian Critical Infrastructure Intrusion campaign represents a highly complex and targeted cyber assault on water and wastewater systems. Employing a multi-stage approach, the attackers meticulously gather intelligence, develop resources, and infiltrate networks to achieve strategic disruption. Their expertise allows them to persistently operate within the environment, escalating privileges, evading defenses, accessing credentials, and exfiltrating sensitive data. Ultimately, the attackers aim to impact critical operations, potentially leading to physical harm or significant service interruptions.
